description: Shimamura reflects on their truant life in the gym—the stagnation, the silence, and the unexpected moment Adachi suggests playing ping-pong.
content:
When I first entered junior high, I thought I'd already become an adult. That illusion didn't last long. I soon realized I wasn't good at dealing with people.

Maybe that was just an excuse I made up—something to justify running away from the noisy mess called 'human relationships.'

Now I'm in high school, and I've taken that habit a step further. I'm risking never growing up at all. I flee from the classroom that pretends to be perfectly orderly, from the lectures that are supposed to help my future, from the classmates who all sit upright like good students… and hide on the second floor of the gym—together with Adachi.

Summer has already passed, but the place is still humid and stuffy. Even moving my shoulders makes heat collect inside my uniform, sticking to my skin. Ever since we started skipping class, I feel as if even the seasons have stopped moving with me. My fingertips trace across the poorly cleaned wooden floor, feeling tiny grains of dust.

It was in that kind of place, in that kind of air, that I met Adachi. A lazy little encounter at the tail end of summer. After that, we stuck around the gym like moss clinging to a rock.

'Adachi.'

'Mm.'

The moment we speak, warm stagnant air slips into our throats, making them dry. But we're too lazy to even drink, so the conversation naturally dies down. If anyone asks what a true slacker looks like, that would be us.

'Do your parents know you're skipping class?'

Adachi turns her head toward the wall. 'Who knows… We don't really talk at home.'

She answered with a detached calmness, as if commenting on scenery across a river—her words never quite reaching her heart. Maybe her bond with her family is similarly thin, distant. Even when they share the same space, they don't talk about their lives, and the distance between them never narrows. And it's clear she has no intention of bridging it.

Being in the same tank doesn't mean the fish inside are close.

We have a fish tank at home too. My little sister takes care of it.

…But do fish really ignore the other fish beside them? Do they really have no desire to get closer? No matter how long you stare at the tank, you can't see their thoughts.

'What about you?' she asks back, almost politely.

'I got caught ages ago. Now Mom pokes my head and nags me nonstop.' She even stopped making my lunch. And when I dyed my hair, everyone just yelled at me for it.

'Oh…'

Her quiet response ends the conversation—just like always.

But if I wanted cheerful chatting with classmates, I would've stayed in the classroom.

It's quiet here, but not like winter quiet where snow absorbs every sound. This silence has... another texture. Like we're deliberately ignoring noises that haven't really faded. It's not exactly peaceful. My heart stays restless.

I rub my skirt between my fingers, then exhale softly.

Mm… well, whatever—I almost say that aloud.

It's comfortable here, but staying still forever will get suffocating again. And I don't mean teachers will find us—it's something far worse: boredom.

When things stop moving, they get stagnant. Stagnant becomes dirty. Dirty sinks.

If I don't want that, I have to move. Even if it's just spinning my wheels, changing nothing.

I look up, and my eyes land on the legs of an old ping-pong table. It looks abandoned.

This school no longer has a table tennis club. If it did, we probably never would've met here.

At that moment, Adachi stands up. I think she's leaving, but her bag is still there. She goes to the far corner of the gym. I just watch quietly, curious.

She returns holding a cardboard box.

'What's that?'

'Paddles and balls.'

'Paddles?'

She sets the box on the table. I get up and peek inside—sure enough, it's ping-pong stuff. Paddles, balls, a net, clips. Everything you need. Dust flies everywhere after being untouched for so long. One breath could probably kill us.

I wave my hand to clear the dust. Adachi finishes setting up and asks: 'Wanna play?'

'…Ping-pong?'

'Why are you saying it in English?'

'No reason… Wait, is that how English says it too?'

I thought 'ping-pong' was just a nickname.

'…Who knows.'

We're truant idiots with terrible grades—this is about our level.

Still, her suggestion surprises me. I mean, it involves moving. And frankly, I don't know her well enough to decide whether her behavior is unusual.

Maybe she felt the same creeping boredom, and wanted to move too. We both wandered all over the school before ending up in the same hiding place. Maybe our senses really are similar.

No wonder being with her doesn't feel bad.

At this moment I finally realize why she feels comfortable to be around.

And her choice of activity suits me perfectly—Not going outside, not leaving the second floor, just using the table right in front of us.

This… level of effort?

This… warmth?

It matches me well.

'Ah… but if you don't want to, it's fine,' she says, shrinking a little when I don't answer. Normally I'd be lazy and refuse—but this time, since I already stood up…

Well, why not.

Even a small stone can make you stumble. Even losing rock-paper-scissors can make you go farther than expected. The reason doesn't matter.

'Sure. Let's play.'

Before boredom swallows us again.

'Whoa, the paddle feels slippery.'

'Yeah, slippery.'

We stand on opposite sides of the table. Adachi faces the gym, I face the windows.

'Heh, isn't the sunlight glaring?' I grin smugly from the shady side.

'Ah… true.' She squints as the sun hits her face. Her long lashes tremble protectively.

'Well, I'll let you have that advantage,' she says with a soft smile.

Maybe she's actually good at this—she was the one who suggested it.

Still, the heat behind me is awful. Summer's over, so why is it this hot? Is someone holding a magnifying glass behind me?

As I swipe my hair, I turn around dramatically toward the light. Watching the bright sky from within the dim gym, I feel a strange sense of yearning. The clouds drifting in the distance carry the scent of wind and the end of summer.

'Shimamura, I scored.'

I turn back, then follow the direction she points. A colorful ping-pong ball bounces toward the wall.

I hurry to pick it up, take a deep breath, and swing my paddle around in an exaggerated wind-up.

'What are you doing?' Adachi laughs.

Good question… but maybe this is me trying to break the stagnation. To change, even slightly.

Change… change… Okay, then I'll hit a curveball. No idea how—just that it must curve in the air.

I place the ball on my palm and focus.

Outside, the late-summer cicadas keep screaming—the lingering echo of summer.
